questions.stem: Why is classification of organisms needed?

options.text: Because of the immense diversity of species
To reduce the number of species

questions.explanation: Classification is needed because of the immense diversity of species; it also facilitates further study.

questions.stem: Which is the correct order of the traditional taxonomic hierarchy (broadest to narrowest)?

options.text: Kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species
Species, genus, family, order, class, phylum, kingdom (broadest first)
Kingdom, class, phylum, family, order, species, genus
Genus, species, kingdom, order, class, phylum, family

questions.explanation: The traditional hierarchy runs kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species from broadest to narrowest.

questions.stem: Why is a fixed ranking of taxa (kingdom, phylum, etc.) considered arbitrary?

options.text: It does not reflect the gradation of variation produced by evolution

questions.explanation: Fixed ranks are arbitrary because they do not reflect the gradual variation generated by evolution.

questions.stem: Cladistics offers an alternative to traditional ranks by using:
questions.explanation: Cladistics uses unranked clades rather than a fixed hierarchy of ranks, representing a paradigm shift.

questions.stem: An advantage of classifying organisms according to evolutionary relationships is that:

options.text: Characteristics of members of a group can be predicted

questions.explanation: When a group shares a common ancestor, characteristics of its members can be predicted from shared clade features.

questions.stem: A clade is a group of organisms that:

options.text: Live in the same place only

questions.explanation: A clade is a group of organisms with common ancestry and shared characteristics.

questions.stem: The most objective evidence for placing organisms in the same clade comes from:

options.text: Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins

questions.explanation: Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins provide the most objective evidence for clade membership.

questions.scenario: Sequence differences accumulate gradually over time.
questions.stem: This principle is used in the technique known as the:
questions.explanation: The gradual accumulation of sequence differences is used as a molecular clock to estimate divergence times.

questions.stem: The molecular clock can only give estimates because mutation rates are affected by factors such as:

options.text: Generation time, population size and selective pressure

questions.explanation: Mutation rates vary with generation time, population size, intensity of selection and other factors, so the clock gives only estimates.

questions.scenario: A cladogram is constructed from molecular data.
questions.stem: Which analysis selects the most probable cladogram using the fewest sequence changes?
questions.explanation: Parsimony analysis chooses the cladogram that explains the observed variation with the smallest number of changes.

questions.scenario: On a cladogram, a branch point where lineages split is shown.
questions.stem: What is this branch point called?
questions.explanation: A node is a branch point on a cladogram, representing a hypothetical common ancestor.

questions.stem: On a cladogram, a node represents:
questions.explanation: Each node on a cladogram represents a hypothetical common ancestor of the lineages branching from it.

questions.stem: The base of a cladogram, representing the common ancestor of all groups shown, is called the:
questions.explanation: The root is the base of the cladogram, representing the common ancestor of all the organisms shown.

questions.scenario: The figwort family (Scrophulariaceae) was reclassified after cladistic analysis.
questions.stem: What does this case study illustrate?

options.text: Cladistics can reveal that a traditional classification was incorrect

questions.explanation: The figwort reclassification shows cladistics correcting classifications based on convergent similarities rather than ancestry.

questions.scenario: Two plants looked similar and were grouped together, but cladistics showed they were not closely related.
questions.stem: The misleading similarity was most likely due to:
questions.explanation: Similarity from convergent evolution, not common ancestry, can lead to false classifications that cladistics corrects.

questions.stem: The classification of all organisms into three domains was based on evidence from:
questions.explanation: The three-domain system was proposed using evidence from ribosomal RNA (rRNA) base sequences.

questions.stem: The three-domain classification added a new taxonomic level:
questions.explanation: The three domains form an extra taxonomic level above kingdoms, proposed in 1977.

questions.scenario: The shift from fixed ranks to unranked clades changed how biologists classify life.
questions.stem: This is best described as an example of:
questions.explanation: Moving to cladistics represents a paradigm shift, a fundamental change in scientific approach.

questions.stem: An 'ideal' classification is one in which all members of a taxonomic group:

options.text: Have evolved from a common ancestor
Have the same number of cells

questions.explanation: The ideal classification follows evolutionary relationships, so group members share a common ancestor.

questions.stem: Besides molecular sequences, what else can be used to assign organisms to clades?
questions.explanation: Morphological traits can be used to assign organisms to clades, though sequences are the most objective evidence.

questions.scenario: Two possible cladograms explain the same sequence data.
questions.stem: Parsimony analysis would favour the one that:
questions.explanation: Parsimony selects the cladogram accounting for the data with the smallest number of sequence changes.

questions.stem: A terminal branch on a cladogram represents:

options.text: An individual group or species at the tip

questions.explanation: A terminal branch ends at a tip, representing a specific group or species being classified.

questions.stem: The more differences there are between the sequences of two clades, the:

options.text: Longer ago they are estimated to have diverged

questions.explanation: Greater accumulated sequence difference implies a longer time since divergence, the basis of the molecular clock.

questions.stem: After organisms are classified, one benefit is that it:

options.text: Facilitates a broad range of further study

questions.explanation: Classification facilitates a broad range of further study once it is completed.

questions.scenario: A previously accepted classification is shown to be wrong by cladistic evidence.
questions.stem: This demonstrates that scientific knowledge claims:
questions.explanation: Theories and knowledge claims may eventually be falsified, as when cladistics overturns a morphological classification.

questions.scenario: Two species share nearly identical gene sequences.
questions.stem: This is strong evidence that they:
questions.explanation: Highly similar sequences are objective evidence of common ancestry, placing organisms in the same clade.

questions.stem: The three-domain system was proposed in which year?
questions.explanation: The revolutionary three-domain reclassification was proposed in 1977.

questions.stem: Why does the traditional hierarchy of taxa sometimes fail to match evolutionary patterns?

options.text: Evolutionary divergence does not fit into fixed, equal ranks
All organisms diverge at the same rate

questions.explanation: The fixed ranks do not correspond to the uneven, gradual divergence produced by evolution.

questions.stem: Cladograms can be constructed from:

options.text: Base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins
Only the size of the organism

questions.explanation: Cladograms are built from base sequences of genes or amino acid sequences of proteins.

questions.stem: Why can the molecular clock give only estimates rather than exact dates?

options.text: Mutation rates are not perfectly constant

questions.explanation: Because mutation rates vary with several factors, the molecular clock provides estimates, not exact dates.

questions.scenario: A student reads a cladogram to find which species share the most recent common ancestor.
questions.stem: The most recent common ancestor of two species is found at:

options.text: The nearest node linking their branches

questions.explanation: The most recent common ancestor of two species is the nearest node where their lineages meet.

questions.scenario: A newly discovered species is placed in a clade whose members share a particular enzyme.
questions.stem: What can be predicted about the new species?

options.text: It is likely to share that characteristic
It must be from another kingdom

questions.explanation: Because clade members share characteristics from common ancestry, features of a new member can be predicted.

questions.stem: Why are molecular sequences considered more objective than morphology for defining clades?

options.text: They are less affected by convergent similarities and subjective judgement

questions.explanation: Base or amino acid sequences give objective data, whereas morphology can mislead through convergent evolution.

questions.stem: Cladistics can be used to investigate whether a traditional group:
questions.explanation: Cladistics tests whether classifications correspond to actual evolutionary relationships.

questions.stem: An 'unranked clade' differs from a traditional taxon because it:

options.text: Is not assigned a fixed rank like family or order

questions.explanation: Clades are defined by common ancestry without being forced into a fixed rank in a hierarchy.

questions.scenario: Different judgement criteria can lead to different cladograms.
questions.stem: This illustrates that in science:

options.text: Different criteria for judgement can lead to different hypotheses
There is only ever one possible hypothesis

questions.explanation: Different criteria can yield different hypotheses; parsimony is one criterion for choosing the most probable cladogram.

questions.stem: Ribosomal RNA sequences were useful for defining the domains because rRNA is:

options.text: Present and comparable across all organisms
Different in every cell of one organism

questions.explanation: rRNA occurs in all organisms, so its sequences can be compared across all life to establish the domains.

questions.stem: Classification helps biologists mainly by:

options.text: Organising the immense diversity of life for study
Reducing the number of living species

questions.explanation: Classification organises the vast diversity of species, making further study manageable.

questions.scenario: A student must deduce clades from a cladogram.
questions.stem: A clade on a cladogram consists of:

options.text: An ancestor and all of its descendants

questions.explanation: A clade comprises a common ancestor (a node) together with all descendants branching from it.

questions.scenario: A species with a very short generation time may accumulate mutations faster.
questions.stem: This affects the molecular clock by:

options.text: Changing the rate at which sequence differences build up

questions.explanation: Generation time influences mutation rate, so it affects how quickly the molecular clock 'ticks', giving estimates only.

questions.stem: Classification that follows evolutionary relationships is often described as:

options.text: Natural, because it reflects common ancestry

questions.explanation: Classifications reflecting common ancestry are considered natural and allow characteristics to be predicted.

questions.stem: Organisms in the same clade are expected to share characteristics because they:

options.text: Inherited them from a common ancestor

questions.explanation: Shared characteristics within a clade come from common ancestry.

questions.stem: In the figwort case study, students are:

options.text: Not required to memorise the details
Required to memorise every species involved
Told the case study is fictional

questions.explanation: The figwort family reclassification illustrates the idea, but students are not required to memorise its details.

questions.stem: The three domains of life are:
questions.explanation: The three-domain system, from rRNA evidence, divides life into Bacteria, Archaea and Eukaryota.

questions.stem: Which statement about cladistics versus traditional classification is correct?

options.text: Cladistics uses unranked clades rather than fixed ranks

questions.explanation: Cladistics offers an alternative using unranked clades, unlike the fixed ranks of traditional classification.

questions.stem: The principle of parsimony assumes that the most likely evolutionary path is the one with:
questions.explanation: Parsimony favours the explanation requiring the smallest number of changes.

questions.stem: Which set of terms is correctly associated with cladograms?
questions.explanation: Cladograms are described using the terms root, node and terminal branch.

questions.stem: A classification that groups organisms sharing a common ancestor allows biologists to:
questions.explanation: Grouping by common ancestry lets characteristics of members be predicted from shared clade features.

questions.scenario: Cladistic evidence contradicts a long-accepted plant grouping.
questions.stem: The appropriate scientific response is to:

options.text: Revise the classification in light of the new evidence
Ignore the evidence to keep the old grouping

questions.explanation: When evidence falsifies a classification, it should be revised, as happened with the figwort family.

questions.stem: The molecular clock relies on the assumption that sequence differences accumulate:
questions.explanation: The molecular clock assumes gradual accumulation of sequence differences, allowing divergence times to be estimated.
